{{Short description|Energy stored in an object due to its position or configuration}}
{{Infobox physical quantity
| name = Potential energy
| image = Mediaeval archery reenactment.jpg
| caption = A drawn bow stores elastic potential energy that is converted to kinetic energy upon release.
| symbols = <math>U</math>, <math>V</math>, <math>E_p</math>
| SI_unit = [[joule]] (J)
}}

In [[physics]], '''potential energy''' is the [[energy]] held by an object because of its position relative to other objects, stresses within itself, its electric charge, or other factors. The term ''potential energy'' was introduced by [[William John Macquorn Rankine]] in the 19th century.

Potential energy is associated with [[force]]s that depend on the position or configuration of an object. If the [[Work (physics)|work]] done by a force on a body that moves from an initial to a final position is determined only by these two positions and does not depend on the path taken, then the force is called [[conservative force|conservative]].

== Relation to conservative forces ==
For a conservative force <math>\mathbf{F}</math>, the potential energy function <math>U(\mathbf{r})</math> is defined such that:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U</math>

In one dimension:
:<math>F(x) = -\frac{dU}{dx}</math>

The work done by a conservative force equals the negative change in potential energy:
:<math>W_{\text{cons}} = -\Delta U = U_i - U_f</math>

== Types of potential energy ==

=== Gravitational potential energy ===
{{Main|Gravitational energy}}
Near Earth's surface (uniform field):
:<math>U_g = mgh</math>

General form (Newtonian gravity) between two point masses:
:<math>U_g = -G\frac{m_1 m_2}{r}</math>
where <math>G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{N·m}^2/\text{kg}^2</math>.

=== Elastic potential energy ===
{{Main|Elastic energy}}
For an ideal spring obeying [[Hooke's law]] (<math>F = -kx</math>):
:<math>U_s = \frac{1}{2}kx^2</math>

=== Electrostatic potential energy ===
{{Main|Electric potential energy}}
Between two point charges:
:<math>U_e = k_e\frac{q_1 q_2}{r}</math>
where <math>k_e = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}</math> is the [[Coulomb constant]].

=== Chemical potential energy ===
The potential energy stored in the arrangement of atoms within molecules; released or absorbed during [[chemical reaction]]s.

=== Nuclear potential energy ===
The energy stored in the [[atomic nucleus]]; released in [[nuclear fission]] and [[nuclear fusion]].

== Conservation of mechanical energy ==
{{Main|Conservation of energy}}
In a system with only conservative forces, the total [[mechanical energy]] is conserved:
:<math>E = K + U = \text{constant}</math>

When non-conservative forces (e.g., friction) are present:
:<math>W_{\text{nc}} = \Delta K + \Delta U = \Delta E</math>

== Equilibrium and stability ==
For a potential energy function <math>U(x)</math>:
* '''Stable equilibrium''' — <math>\frac{dU}{dx} = 0</math> and <math>\frac{d^2U}{dx^2} > 0</math> (potential minimum)
* '''Unstable equilibrium''' — <math>\frac{dU}{dx} = 0</math> and <math>\frac{d^2U}{dx^2} < 0</math> (potential maximum)
* '''Neutral equilibrium''' — <math>\frac{dU}{dx} = 0</math> and <math>\frac{d^2U}{dx^2} = 0</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Kinetic energy]]
* [[Work (physics)]]
* [[Conservative force]]
* [[Energy]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Potential energy| ]]
[[Category:Energy]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
